The Japanese version of Mega Man 7 has Mega Man stop charging his buster after Wily reminds him that he cannot harm humans; which effects this theory significantly, as it suggests Mega Man's immunity to the three laws of Robotics was added in the English version. The stuff with the Mega Man 9 robots still applies, however, but keep in mind that Dr. Wily also modifies them to better suit his needs. The Mega Man X series establishes that the first completely autonomous robot was Mega Man X, establishing that all previous robots at least had some limitation on their autonomy, like the Three Laws (this is why Proto Man is not considered a Reploid like the robots in Mega Man X are).

There's few small flaws in your theory, Matt.

1. Dr. Wily is hardly an idiot when it comes to robotics. You make it sound like all he can do is sit back and beg robots to pretty please join him. If that were the case the stages and his fortresses would be empty, and he'd come at you on a bicycle.

2. You made it sound like Dr. Light made all the robot Masters. He only made the first and ninth Batch of Robot Masters himself. Has I recall, the second, fifth and Seventh batches were all made from the ground up by Wily. The third was made by Light and Wily together. The fourth batch was made by Dr. Cossack. The sixth were made by robotic engineers from all over the world and entered in a global competition. The eighth also came from all sorts of different places, such as Clown Man's being an old amusement park attraction, though I don't believe their creators are ever credited none of them were Thomas Light, I'm pretty such.

3. LIGHT'S ROBOT'S CAN'T KILL PEOPLE.
Dr. Wily reprograms them and erases the program that prevents them from doing so. Yes this includes Mega Man. That's probably why he's talking so slow he's, trying to go against his programing, and probably wouldn't have been able to. Remember that scene in Robocop, when he tries to kill Dick Jones? Yeah he was a real prick too. But Robocop could not break his programing, and that dude had a Human Brain! It was like that. Mega can point the Buster and threaten. But that's about it. As advance as Mega Man's A.I. was, able to make some choices for himself he was still a slave to his programing.
That's what makes X so special. Dr. Light wanted him to be a real person, he wanted a son. So he made something more than a robot, something with complete free will, capable of random thoughts and actions, true autonomy. An absolute variable, hence the name "X". And though he did it begrudgingly, that included the ability to kill people. He knew he had to have that choice for his actions and decisions to have any meaning.
If what you're saying is true, then X's existence is pointless because Dr. Light's been pumping these out since day one.

If I remembered correctly, the robots were not created with weapons.

They had tools, but are later modified by Wily and turned into weapon grade arsenals.  Almost all of the robots were industrial robots, except for Rock, Roll, and Zero.  The fact that Wily mentioned that robots cannot harm humans, it means Light must have already had safety protocols similar to Asimov's laws in those other robots, except for Megaman who were meant to be as human as possible.

Megaman's statement is actually an assertion of his ability to be able to judge what is right and wrong.  Meaning he is not a slave to humanity, but as equals.

BTW Asimov's laws are not air tight because a robot cannot judge what is considered harm or injury without very specific parameter.  In fact the first law is the problem, because while it prevents a robot rebellion, it will make the robot non-functional quite often, which will be explained below.

Okay, let's say I am in a mall with a gun pointed at my head by a lunatic who is about to shoot me. The first law dictates that the robots in the vicinity must help me, but the first law also stated that harming the guy holding a gun to my head is not allowed.

Now a normal person with freewill will assess the situation and make a decision. Which decision or whether it is a good decision or not, it doesn't really matter.  What matters is that a person with freewill can look at the other decision and say "screw it".  However, a robot cannot do such things because it has no freewill due to Asimov's law.

Lastly, the example about the autonomous turret.  Is that suppose to be horrifying because the turret has freewill, or is it horrifying that it has the ability to kill without human pulling the trigger?  Yet, the turret have no freewill and is following programming set by humans, essentially an obedient slave.  Freewill is not the issue with that turret, it is how horrifying human beings can be when we act in fear that is the problem.  The turret is only doing what we told it to do, and we did not give it freewill to question the orders.

Freewill can be good or bad, and if we deem robots as loyal servants, then we first must question ourselves because we are the ones with the freewill to do so, not the robots.

Do you know why Dr. Light made X? He did it to have complete free will on his actions, not before putting him in a 30 years A.I. Test to assure he didn't have homicide thoughts to the human race. All this happening in the end of the Classic Megaman timeline

Fun video, and an interesting little hypothesis. However, there are some problems with it:

1: Dr Light did NOT create all the Robot Masters in each game. Dr Wily created the ones in Mega Man 2, 5, 7, and 8, Dr Cossack created the ones in 4, and several scientists were the creators of the robots in Mega Man 6. Since so many of these robots were made by other people, you can't say that it was all Dr Light's fault that they went on a rampage. And keep in mind, Wily created many of these himself, making him share even more of the responsibility.

2: It is heavily suggested by the overall plot that the type of autonomy Mega Man possesses is unique to him. Notice in the very scene that you use to prove your argument, Wily is shocked that Mega Man is able to decide for himself to blast him.. and this is in Mega Man 7, meaning that a whole lot of time has passed with Wily and Light messing around with robotics. If autonomy was something that all these robots had, why then is Wily surprised that Mega Man was able to make the decision he made? Even more, consider also that the ones in Mega Man 9 were going to accept their fates, and only went with Wily because he promised to fix them so they could keep on doing their intended purposes (by the way, note that many of these Masters are actually re-purposed utility/worker robots, only sticking with the jobs assigned and programed to them. What's more, the decision making abilities they have were specific to their job set and for commanding other less advanced robots for the projects they were made for  - Guts Man, a construction robot, commanding a bunch of less advanced construction bots such as the mets - ; that's why they are called Robot Masters, actually. If they had the full autonomy Mega Man has, couldn't they have decided to do some other work, or be able to go against their programing?). They did NOT chose to go on a rampage, that only happened after he reprogrammed them. So if the autonomy that Mega Man possesses is unique to him, or at the very least is something that only a few of the robots have (such as Proto Man, Roll, and Bass), then that pretty much deflates the argument that all these rampages are Dr Light's fault because of all these robots having autonomy.

Another point, though it's not really theory busting: Dr Light didn't actually just let Proto Man run off and not care about pursuing him. When Proto Man escaped, he was considered destroyed, thus there was no attempt to get him back. Dr wily later found and repaired him; that's how he was able to figure out how to reprogram the robots Dr Light made.

So yeah, nice hypothesis, but it doesn't really hold up when one recalls the overall plot of the games. Still was a fun video though, and I like the other Game Theorists videos, so no biggie. :p

The scene where Megaman decides to kill Wily actually is covered under Asimov's laws, because there's an unwritten "zeroth" law, which supersedes all the others. "A robot may not harm HUMANITY, or through inaction allow HUMANITY to come to harm." If by killing one man a robot saves many lives, then the robot is not just ALLOWED to do so, it MUST do so. This is a dilemma Asimov himself addressed. So, it's actually possible that Megaman was following the laws of robotics. By killing Wily Megaman could save countless lives, so under the zeroth law he actually is required to kill the man. He's just following his programming.

Dr Light wasn't intentionally evil, but much like many of our geniuses. A rough excerpt of my Grandmother Amy's dinner conversations,
"I once went into a Chemistry Class (I think she was talking about her college years.) and the teacher there was BRILLIANT. He had a passion for his craft.
He had NO common-sense, what-so-ever!"

Like Albert Einstein, deep in thought about his high level science with his wife yelling at him to come back and put on his pants before he goes to work, Light was hyper smart, but had little common sense.

Thus, we have human stupidity in a nutshell. No matter how high your IQ is, you are gonna be stupid one way or another.

As someone who's into astrophysics, I ran some of MatPat's numbers through some orbital mechanics calculations.  I did this twice, with the first calculation assuming that the moon's composition (due to having a much lower albedo than the actual moon) would be analogous to an asteroid similar to Ryugu with a low albedo.  The second calculation assumed it has the same composition as the actual moon.  Both used 63.5m as the size.

Calculation 1 found that aerodynamic ram pressure upon hitting the upper atmosphere of Termina at a velocity resulting in a fall from the minimum stable orbit would result in catastrophic breakup, with the stress upon the material holding the rock together being at or around 30Pa at only 100km above the surface with Ryugu having an estimated strength of 10Pa.  The result of this?  Roughly analogous to an airburst detonation of around 2 megatons.  A little less powerful than the ACTUAL Tunguska Event from 1908.  Clock Town and all surrounding buildings and trees would be absolutely flattened by the pressure wave, and anything flammable would likely catch fire.

Calculation 2 resulted in even more concerning numbers.
Assuming the moon is the same composition of the actual moon, it makes it through the atmosphere and actually reaches the ground instead of breaking in mid-air.  This is where things get interesting.  The moon would impact Clock Town traveling at around 7.2km/s, and that's on the low end of things.  The result would be equivalent to slightly over 6 megatons.  The resulting crater (because nothing in Clock Town survives that kind of impact) would be around 750m across, and over 100m deep.  The impact would also cause a magnitude 6 earthquake.

While it wouldn't destroy the planet, the impact of the moon in this game would be DEVASTATING.  Tidal forces actually wouldn't be that bad with an object that small.  It's actually so small in fact, that it wouldn't have enough mass to reach its Roche Limit and be torn apart by Termina's gravity before atmospheric entry.

It's fascinating regardless!

If the planet link is on (presumably based on earth but different) is small enough for that moon to make differences to tide (even though he did use the standard estimate for the energy to destroy OUR Earth) perhaps the moon would be able to destroy it. If the planet link is on is standard Earth size, it would be fair to assume that the moon is too small to be effected by tide FROM THIS MOON (keep in mind, high tide never happened in the game, even though the moon was nearly crashing into earth. You could stand next to the coast all year and nothing would happen. Therefore, it would be fair to assume that the moon is really really small, and the Earth is standard Earth size. Also, gravity is magic in that world (as a separate universe it is very likely that the laws of Physics would be nothing like ours). This is proven when the gravity flips in the Stone Tower, but we are told by the Garo Master that all this is doing is flipping the placement of the moon and earth. This means that the center of gravity in this universe is intrinsic to certain points (and since none of the physics change we can assume that distance doesn't affect gravity) and that the celestial bodies are immune to gravity, as Termina doesn't immediately fall into the moon after they switch, despite its massive mass. (You may think that the moon falling to Termina disproves this, but remember, Majora had to push the moon toward Termina, it wasn't falling, it was being forced to move by magic.) And that small of a moon wouldn't have had such a disastrous effect on the weather. And this solar system may just be Geocentric as the stars make light without nuclear fusion (star fragments). In other words, the physics are different, so you can't compare things using our scientific laws.

Silly nitpick, but if you watch the game over cutscene, you'll see that the moon actually explodes before it even touches the ground. It's this explosion that destroys everything around it and kills Link, not the impact. The same sort of thing has happened during some asteroid “collisions” on Earth, where the incoming asteroid actually explodes in the atmosphere before even reaching the ground.

It still doesn't make a huge amount of sense in the grand scheme of things (the moon in Majora doesn’t have anywhere near the energy required for that), but it could maybe at least explain the destruction somewhat. Nuclear bombs are often designed to detonate before they hit the ground, to extend the blast radius. Maybe if we assume that Majora himself triggers the explosion that destroys termina from inside the moon then the resulting damage makes a little more sense; you’re then effectively looking at a 450 kiloton bomb rather than a 450000 ton piece of rock.

It’s still completely absurd, but perhaps just slightly less absurd. XD

The thing that these folks have apparently overlooked is the fact that a "moon" of its mass would not have enough gravitational force to cause the sort of apocalyptic conditions that they've described.

To put this in perspective, I've calculated the force of the Terminian moon based on the numbers provided in this video:

2.97 x 10⁻⁵ N

For those of you who don't know scientific notation, that's a _fucking small number_. There's a reason why only massive objects have enough of a gravitational pull to make any real difference - gravity is by far the weakest fundamental force in the entire universe. It is directly proportionate to the inertial mass of an object. That creepy-@ss thing in the Terminian skyline does not have enough girth to annihilate virtually anything.

There's a flaw to the moon climate thing. The Zelda moon's mass. Considering the insane lack of mass, the Termina moon is more of a dense ass orbital meteorite, and this moon's shift in distance would have little overall affect on the earth sized world Hyrule exists on (if we consider your game connection theory).

Secondly, the slow moon fall thing is nothing more than Nintendo needing an ominous game over screen. At that speed, it's more likely that Majora is holding the moon up with his power, instead of pulling it.

One of the ways I’ve interpreted Majora’s Mask is that at the end of Ocarina of Time Link defeats Gannon, saves the Princess, saves the world and becomes a child again. He pretty much has fulfilled his destiny by the age of 10; anyone in that situation would wonder to themselves- “What purpose does my life have now, what’s left?” So I view Link’s journey in Majora’s Mask is him searching for the meaning of his existence in a new land and by collecting numerous masks that have their own unique design as well as different powers. This can be seen as a metaphor for Link having an identity crisis and part of this journey is a quest for him to find himself. But that’s just my theory.

You missed something rather important. The chronology of the number "4" throughout Majora's Mask. You use four forms for most of the game's puzzles, there are four major story arcs/dungeons, there are four giants, etc. etc.  -- in Japan, the number "4" is heavily symbolic of death. It's notable that the beginning of the fourth day is game over *except* at the very end of the game -- when Link has accepted his own death. But it's just a theory...!

This video was impactful in my life. I got into the whole of The Legend of Zelda because of it. It was at a time just after school where I was bullied and overworked. I didn't even remember what I enjoyed doing and Majora's Mask reminded me playing video games was one of those things.

I came back to the video because I remember it filled my head with expectations of deep messages about death which Majora's Mask didn't really have even if it was so good. And it is now that I'm playing Persona 3 and finally it explores these themes with excruciating detail.

Play it if you have the chance. The story is illuminating

I have something kinda dumb to just kind of put into the puzzle here. Okay, so while the humans are waiting underground for the nuclear fallout to subside, the climate begins to change. In other words, we're hit by a nuclear winter, lasting only about 5-15 years maybe. The planet is pretty much frozen over during this time however. But during this time, Earth is visited by a little pink ball we all know and love: Kirby. In Kirby and the Crystal Shards, some of the crystal shards happen to land on a little frozen planet named Shiver Star. Upon closer examination, one can clearly make out the continents on Earth when you look at Shiver Star on the map. It even has a single moon orbiting it! He lands somewhere around the outskirts of a major metropolitan city. It could be a number of cities, but i'm thinking New York City or Tokyo. He then enters a factory containing what appears to be living animals trapped inside of test tubes. I can only assume that perhaps humanity began to turn to genetic engineering in an attempt to solve the energy crisis, (and new bio weaponry perhaps?) but alas, weren't given enough time. Kirby then enters the city and is greeted by a rougue robot that was apparently never shut down, HR-H. The robot is then destroyed, and kirby continues on his merry way, and curiously enough, uneffected by the radiation. 

That's just my little piece of the puzzle that I thought was worth noting.

Remember, we're not talking about ordinary human beings here, either.

1) These are people who can get stabbed, shot multiple times, and then go hide in a bush or underwater and completely regenerate their health in two seconds.
2) They can see shit with Eagle Vision and mark targets from far away (known as "the sense").
3) Edward had no formal assassin training whatsoever and he killed a seasoned assassin after being shipwrecked, exhausted, and shot in the shoulder. He could also climb just as good as any assassin before or after him (that we know of) because "every finger's a fish hook".
4) Most, if not all of them, can leap from the top of a building and land on their target, killing it, and suffer no damage whatsoever.

After all of that, jumping from a tower and landing in a stack of palm leaves doesn't seem so far fetched (compared to everything else).

When doing your original calculation for the time it took to fall in game did you consider that it's not the gravity that's wrong, it's the player's perception of time as the developers do their best to simulate a phenomenon known as Tachypsychia.  Basically the person experiencing the fall would also perceive everything slowing down because of an adrenaline rush.  Don't forget that developers do their best to get down little details like that to make their game seem real and make you feel more like you're the character as opposed to merely playing a game.  It's those little details that will probably make more immersive gaming technology pretty cool... and frightening in some cases.

One possible flaw: How do we know that game time vs real time has a ratio of 1:1 during the fall(s)?
I've seen some games that sped up game-time during some scenes to prevent them from overstaying their welcome due to their non-interactivity. (EG:"30 seconds for this cut-scene? Speed it up to 20, that way it'll end before they quit out of boredom.")

That said, YOU are ultimately responsible at the end of the day. A network, while it may help you to monetize, is NOT going to protect you should someone sue. 

And 9,999 times our of 10,000 you won't get sued. The company will just not allow you to make money from it or will take the money for themselves (AKA Nintendo and my Mario is Communist video). 

But not all game companies care. A lot of companies like the free promotion and don't mind you making money from their content.

Copyright is super complicated because YouTube is inconsistent about it. 

"Fair Use" applies to your right to upload a Let's Play video, for example, but doesn't mean you can make money from it. In short, for gamers, you technically should NEVER be able to make money from a video.

That said, belonging to a network means YouTube is off the hook if a game company sues. As such, YouTube is much more likely to allow you to monetize. And some have agreements that get you whitelisted to use gameplay

There's so much more to Chrono Trigger than meets the eye. I'm doing my very first play through of it and god is this game incredible. It doesn't just parallel the bible in terms of the flooding and the mythos, and extermination of humanity, but it questions the very nature of time and destiny itself. In a scene during a sidequest they realize that something else is controlling the time gates. This brings about the question, who's really in control of time? Were your characters ever really changing or history? Or was history already written out that way? Lavos was bound to the same changes you make throughout your journey, so what is Lavos really? If its bound to these same chains of time, what greater purpose does your journey really serve if at the end all of these time lines are erased and changed and undone?

Chrono Trigger is one of the few games that manages to do something few games do. It becomes bigger than itself. It becomes instead of a question about your journey, to a question of the nature of time and the role it plays and the impact we have on the world and that our actions may actually serve some greater unknown purpose far beyond what any of us comprehend. Is it one giant time paradox? If that's the case, and man was meant to eventually discover time travel, does that mean we're all one giant time paradox as well?

So, today I was researching the Roman, Julian, and Gregorian calendar, and, years after I first watched this video, I finally found out why the months of the revolutions are a month before they took place. They didn’t switch to the Gregorian calendar from the Julian calendar until a year after the revolutions. The Julian calendar is about 10 days behind the Gregorian calendar so for the Russians the revolutions were taking place at the end of the month instead of the beginning of the next one. I know no one will see this, but I had to comment it anyway. Thanks for reading!

I have just gone through Quantum Mechanics in university, and +The Game Theory 's superconductor explanation =/= quantum locking. Quantum locking used by Bioshock actually has to do with the uncertainty principle and the wave-particle duality. Just basically the fact that a "particle" acts as as a particle when you measure its position, but when you measure its momentum, it behaves as a wave. Therefore, quantum locking just means continually observing this particle in quick succession so that it acts as particle and you set its position, at least, according to that new study which came out recently.

The motion sickness explanation is sound, to an extent. Your theory relies on the fact that your brain thinks you are moving, but you cannot feel it. This would work with the clouds, however once you stepped inside, you would feel better. Inside, there are no clouds to give the illusion of motion, so anyone feeling sick would simply, step inside for a couple minutes, gain their composure, then step back outside. But hey, that's only a theory. A COMMENT THEORY!

But here's the kicker: Some people are not affected by motion sickness. To use an extreme example, think about totally blind people. Their bodies feel motion but they can't see it, yet you don't see them walking around with barf bags all the time. So there's one cause of motion sickness which doesn't hold true all the time.

Then there's seeing motion but not feeling it. Well, you're a gamer, lot of people are, we see motion all the time without feeling it, even more so with stuff like the oculus rift coming out. Again, gamers, even rift users, don't constantly have barf bags due to it so, again, another cause of motion sickness shown to not always be in play.

And third, when the body feels and sees the motion but they don't sync up in the brain. That is the most common cause for motion sickness in the human population. However, it is not universal, if it was then we'd all get carsick, but the vast majority of people don't. So not everyone is even affected by motion sickness and, those who are unaffected wouldn't have much issue adapting to the constant movement. Those born on the constantly moving platforms, with that being the default way of motion for their bodies, would be more likely to get sick, or at least feel uncomfortable, on solid ground. Never underestimate the potential for the human body to adapt to it's environment.

Think you may want to look into this one a bit more. Here's my rebuttal to the whole motion sickness thing:

Ships.

People can live on ships indefinitely, in motion, going up and down, up and down, the movement of waves, clouds overhead... but they adjust. People could adjust to seeing clouds float by while gently bobbing up and down just like people at sea can adjust to seeing the waves in the ocean. I can see nothing special about Columbia to suggest that the motion sickness caused by floating in the air is any different than the motion sickness caused by being on a ship.

The first time watching this video, I kinda expected Matpat to talk about the field of epigenetics rather than this DNA encoding thing. See, there are these things called epigenetic tags where certain proteins can attach to a creature's DNA and can actually use enzymes to alter it, turning it on or off or even adding new information into it based partially on the life experiences of the creature. The evidence of these life experiences, in the form of these epigenetic tags, is then passed on through DNA replication to the offspring of that creature. It doesn't pass on memories as such, but there have been broader stretches on both this channel and in games as a whole.

To all the people upset with the megabytes to gigabytes calculation, he is actually right.  After enough people got confused by the incorrect use of metric prefixes, new ones were devised.  MB means megabyte, and is now 1000 KB.  1000MB makes a GB now.  The terms you are thinking of are now written as MiB and GiB.  MiB stands for Mebibyte, and is 1024KiB.  1024MiB makes a Gibibyte, or GiB.  So yes, Matt is correct.

Actually, life choices can in fact alter DNA. Not the actual sequence, but how the DNA is wrapped around histones. This is called epigenetics, meaning "above genetics" because it's like a separate layer from the code itself that determines what it says. SciShow has a great video on it, but I'll summarize it. Your DNA is wrapped around proteins called histones, making it more compact. If it's wrapped really tight, that section can't be read by the cell. So how the DNA is arranged can change how it's processed. All through your life, your DNA changes how it's wrapped to increase efficiency. If there's a section that isn't used often, it'll wrap tighter and loosen in an area used more often. When gametes (eggs and sperm) are made, most of this structure is erased, but some parts remain. These small sections are then passed to the child, influencing inheritance. Granted, the effect is no where near on the scale needed to recreate actual memories, but it's still events in your life being passed down the generations.

Despite what many think, DNA is not a fixed construct. A few years ago, there was a study on the DNA of a pair of identical twins. When the twins were born, their DNA strands were exactly the same, but when they both grew up and lead different lives, their DNA was tested again. It turned out that their strands were ever so slightly different from each other. This is because the strands of DNA will become destabilized over time as electromagnetic radiation interferes with the membranes of the cells. This background radiation can come from a variety of sources, such as the sun, radio and cell phone signals, and even the electrical activity of the nervous system itself.

The conversion ratio for different tiers of bytes 1 to 1024. 1 GB is 1024 MB, so the math goes a bit differently. 6.8 GB -> 6,963.2 MB. 6,963.2 * $12,400 = $86,343,680.00, a significant $2,000,000 difference. For decoding, $220 * 6,963.2 = $1,531,904.00, adding a less significant $31,904.00 assuming the price per MB is accurate. The $87,875,584 total cost is ~$1.8 million more expensive than portrayed. I'm going to assume that you rounded strangely, because despite the discrepancies, I've triple checked my calculations. Other than that, this has been informative and enjoyable! Thank you for your work!

Now the question becomes this:
Psychologists don't know where memories are stored in our brain, nor how to get them out, or how they're encoded (think file formats). The fact that you can store word documents into DNA isn't the same as actually storing memories - that's going to take a LOT more research (and probably a few million more dollars).
Frank Herbert's Dune series was predicated on this very concept - long before video games were a thing.

I don't usually do this, but i feel like i have to say something regarding this video.

I'm not going to question the references or mathematics used in the video, i think they're all solid and yes, many of us can fit into a morph ball sized space, but i think with a title like "the metroid morph ball is LAME" you have to discuss its other useful and cool aspects other than just its height reducing capabilities.

I agree, the primary function of the morph ball is to make it so samus can access smaller areas. But even in the first game, the ball has other functions, such as gaining access to the bomb ability which is crucial for exploring. Going past the first game into the prime series the morph ball has many, MANY more useful and cool applications.

I dont know about you, but if i ever tried to roll around as fast as samus does in the prime games, especially with the boost ball upgrade, id probably break my back and neck. The point is the morph ball is meant to enhance samus's MOBILITY, not just reduce her size. This design choice is evident throughout the prime series, acquiring upgrades like the spider ball: gaining the ability to climb walls and travel over magnetic rails, and the aforementioned boost ball allowing her to move MUCH faster than just walking around.

To me, the point of the morph ball was never to allow samus access to places she couldn't fit. Just look at the added mission at the end of metroid: zero mission. Zero suit samus can easily crawl around through places she could only previously access with the morph ball, but at a significantly lower speed, which makes getting away from the pursuing space pirates harder to get away from. In this regard, the morph ball is meant to enhance MOVEMENT, not size.

In conclusion, with a title like that, you really have to consider everything it does for it to be considered lame. To me, a hardcore fan of the series, i can easily see the benefits of the upgrade beyond just the ones discussed in this video. The morph ball is NOT lame, but this video makes it seem like it is. Long story short, look past just whats on the outside next time.

/End Rant

You're wrong.
This is not how the morph ball works. The entire interior of the morph ball is mechanical. When Samus morphs into it, her biological information (along with the suit) is stored within a computer inside the ball. When she morphs back out of the ball, the machine converts the digital information into atoms that re-arrange into the suit with Samus inside of it. This is similar to the Composer in Halo 4, except it can reverse the process also.  Nintendo also released a diagram that shows the interior of the ball. Chozo tech is the shit.

It's been confirmed that the morph ball is 1 meter in diameter. That's about the size of a regular beach ball or disco ball, which I'd love to see someone try and climb into one of those. Also, Samus takes the same amount of damage in the morph ball as out of the morph ball, and out of the morph ball she has all that armor that is also a space suit, so she has to cram all of it into that morph ball and make sure it is able to defend her. I'm sorry but it is quite impressive good sir, once you get your knowledge from official sources, rather than doing the math yourself.

This isn't related to the theory at hand, but I can't find any other thing on here relating to Metroid and samus's power suit but here goes. In order for Samus to work those weapons in her arm cannon like the Ice Beam, Wave Beam etc, it requires her to form some sort of sign gesture appropriate for the weapon she wants to operate, I found this out while playing Metroid prime 1 and observing the amount of detail they included with the x ray visors turned on, and one thing I notice apart from seeing parts of her skeletal under that arm cannon is how when you decide to change weapons the positioning of her hands also rotates to fit the weapons icon below. There might be people on here that already knew that but I had just started getting into Metroid roughly a year or two ago so that was a really big find on my part.

The reason I began gaming (at an early age, 4 or 5) was because of a form of Self-Actualization needs. My community and achievement needs were satisfied by school, but finding myself, dealing with pain and various emotions that were caused by trauma and stress drove me to gaming, to a vast multitude of worlds where I could place myself into the position of a Hero. Overtime, it shaped me, and even allowed me to analyze my true self, fulfilling the need of Self-Actualization. It continues to this day as a form of psycho-therapy and pure enjoyment.

In gaming, I was drawn to things where I could be whatever I wanted, play righteous heroes, or even just derp around for fun. Anything that would take my mind away from the reoccurring pain was welcome, even games that required an immense level of skill. Point being, I was less completionist, and more attached to the games for whatever amount of release I gained from them.

A small part of it for me as well is that I 100% games i love in a weird way of showing that I love them. For instance, I got all the Steam achievements for the entire Dark Souls trilogy, in part because I want to show off, but also in part because I love those games and want to show off that I love them. Sure, there's a drive in me to get every achievement I can in any game, but only in those games I truly enjoy do I actually go all the way to 100%.

actually, the surprise achievement thing is not always quite what you said it is. For instance; when I get the achievement for opening my inventory in minecraft, I was not expecting that, but I don't get any sense of achievement because it was so easy. Now If I say get an achievement for something exceedingly difficult without knowing I did so, that promotes a large sense of achievement. It helps even more if you get unexpected achievements; and those achievements get you or help you to get some in game reward you want; such as a cool in game item. It depends on such factors as these: difficulty of attaining the surprise achievement, and in game reward for getting the achivement.

The music in the Kefka battle is NOT Bach's Toccata and Fugue in D minor. In fact it is not a singular organ piece at all, it is bits and pieces of various Bach organ works combined (within which there might be a tiny section of the Fugue from the aforementioned toccata and fugue)

Also, just because a piece is written for organ, doesn't mean it was written for church purposes. The Toccata and Fugue in D minor is a completely secular piece. (Its authorship is also contested and there are many who believe the work wasn't written by Bach, but that is neither here nor there..)

Could you do a video about the religious symbolism in Kingdom Hearts, particularly 1.The use of Light and Darkness in Kingdom Hearts are a part of its religious symbolism of Christian ideals, and they use "Light" and "Darkness" rather than good and evil or righteousness and sin because its for kids, Disney was a part of it, and political correctness.

Square likes to use a lot of religious symbolism in their games. Square soft isn't against religion in general, but structured religion. I bring this up because they seem to portray religion positively in Kingdom Hearts, 1. 

Heartless, Sora, and Ansem, Seeker of Darkness, are two very profound religious symbols. People become heartless when they allow too much darkness (sin) to enter their heart. In my religion, there's the idea of the "hardening of the heart" where a person becomes numb to emotions good and bad, and in this state they prone to wickedness. The name of "heartless" fits perfectly with this idea. Sora is like Christ. He's the key to light (righteousness). He even sacrifices himself at the end and is reborn. Ansem is Satan. Ultimate goal is to turn consume the world with darkness and turn everyone into heartless (wicked beings),

There seems to be some of  sybolism remaining in the games after KH1 , but I can't think of more than sybolism of repentence with Riku in KH II. Now they seem to be focusing on symbolic societal morals and exposition rather than symbolic religion which I don't mind because  not everyone is religious and I'm already emotionally invested in the characters and the story, so I want to hear the exposition now. ﻿

And eventually, they're at the point where they are strong enough to kill a God because they have become as powerful as that God. I think more than an issue with the idea of organized religion itself, they have problems with blindly trusting in everything a religious leader says for no reason other than they said so. Sonic's "Speed of Sound" reputation is when he's pushing his limits. Keep in mind that Sonic is powered internally by the amount of rings he's gotten in his adventures, so quite possibly, he's able to withstand the G-Force that the speed would bring. Also, Sonic is an avid collector of the infamous Chaos Emeralds and Super Emeralds, enhancing his endurance, speed, and power even further. His power is multiplied by 1000 (according to Sonic X) when he goes Super. Since the speed of sound is 770 MPH and 770 x 1000 = 770,000, that means in Super form, he clocks at a speed of 770,000 MPH, enough to go around the earth 30 times in one hour. But that's when he's pushing his limits. To guess the Blue Blur's average speed, take in account of his height, 3.7, and his weight, 77lb, using the momentum formula of mass and velocity, we'll divide the speed of sound, 770, with his weight, 77 (it conveniently fits perfectly), and get 11. The world record for Green Hill Zone is 25, so we'll do basic multiplication and multiply 11 x 25, and we get Sonic the Hedgehog's average speed of 275 MPH. So, no, maybe not "moving around at the speed of sound", but he still earns the title of the Blue Blur.

Clever, using the building to gauge Sonic's max speed. However, you're forgetting something of an impossibility within the Sonic universe. You see, if we're to take your measurements seriously, then the Choppers, the fish enemies, can fly up to 4.93776 meters in the air before falling back down. And the time it takes from the beginning of its jump to the time it hits the water is about 3 seconds. Now, halve that time... That's 1.5 seconds. So it's moving about 3.29184 m/s, right? Wrong. You see, this Badnik is decelerating on its trip upward, and accelerating back on its way down. 3.29184 m/s is just the initial velocity. So plugging in the initial velocity, and the distance it can travel, that puts its acceleration at about -2.19456 m/s². Why is this significant? Because the Chopper moves *vertically*. Its acceleration... or, should I say its *gravity*... should be -9.8 m/s². That's almost 4.5 times slower than it's supposed to be, and yet Mobius is the same size as Earth, as shown multiple times throughout comics, cartoons, and games. And while the wind resistance could be to blame, it's immediately counteracted with the fact that this thing is made of metal, and that we've seen windy stages since with a noticeable difference in "gravity". So what's the explanation for this? Well, It's not in real time. That's the only possibility, when we remove the impossibilities of Mobius having a different gravity, speculative wind resistance, and boosters on the fish, which it doesn't have. Once you eliminate the impossible, whatever remains must be the truth. So everything that happens in the Sonic levels is happening 4.5 times faster than it'd have us believe. Meaning Sonic's max speed is... let's see, 80 m/s times 4.5 is...

A watermelon and human skull may have the same density but the human skull is definitely tougher to crack. Also a dead person's bones become more brittle as time goes on, so in the WD, for example, killing zombies would definitely be easier.

Everyone tells me the best weapon for the Zombie Apocalypse is either the Shotgun or the Baseball Bat. I have always thought the Fire Axe was the best weapon, and not for the reason you'd think.

Shotgun is good, but can run out of shells, is rather loud, and can be difficult to reload as well as highly inaccurate if your adrenaline is pumping and your hands are shaking. The Baseball Bat is a good club, but has very little in the way of weight, something you will probably need to overcome obstacles.

The Fire Axe has a blunt end, good for smashing in Zombie Skulls. A sharp end, good for cutting through locked doors if you are cornered in a building, and a hooked edge, which can help you get leverage when climbing. Furthermore, the last thing you want to hit Zombies with is a bladed weapon, as they are liable to get stuck, so the Axe's sharp end is not the most effective.

I always have one problem with the many-worlds-theory:
What exactly counts as a decision?
Without this definition, there would be an infinite amount of universes created at the same time. For example a particle could move in an infinite amount of directions with infinite different velocities. If there's a universe for every possibility, then there would be infinitely many universes with each having this particle moving in a different direction with a different velocity. That's not even considering the huge amount of particles there are in the universe.

Your conclusion that the games can't take place in the same branching timeline is based on one assumption: That the events shown in Ocarina of Time is the original variant of that event. This is not a given.
First let's consider the only method, out of all the different modes of timetravel existing in the Zelda universe, which have been shown to be able to split-timelines: OoT's Zelda. Her sending Link back in time at the end of OoT is the thing that created both of the other timelines. Is it not then most likely that her powers are what created the "Downfall" timeline as well?

But we are never shown her do such a thing at any other time in OoT. Since she would have little reason to do so in either the "Child" or "Adult" timelines, the only possibility left is this: She did it before what we are shown in OoT. The events of OoT itself may be a product of time travel.

So who did she send back? Who has most influence on Link's quest? Consider Kaepora Gaebora. He guides Link's direction leading up to him acquiring the Master Sword and he always seems to know more than he is leading on. Consider also an OoT without him: Link might never have gotten the Master Sword. Without it he would have lost against Ganondorf. Without Hirule's legendary hero what would Zelda do? Use her timetravel ability to avert his death.

With Link out of the question Rauru is the most likely person. He would have the most necessary information to make sure Link has what is required to face Ganondorf prepared. Being sent back in time he takes on the form of Kaepora Gaebora (which Hyrule Historia confirm to be him) either to hide his identity from Ganondorf or because his own body is dead at this point. This leads to the events of OoT.

This also makes more sense considering a few other things: All the games prior to OoT takes place in the "Downfall"-timeline and events of ALttP's backstory is recognizably the same as the plot of OoT, but with significant diverges. From this perspective these games are the original timeline while OoT present the “AU”-game.

Also think back to the scene at the start of OoT. We are shown a scene of a young Link confronting Ganondorf. At first look this seem to be a premonition, but the events diverge slightly from how they play out when they actually happen. Maybe this was rather a glimpse from an alternative timeline, the original timeline?

Kirby is in the future of fallout, mutated creature who lives in outer space. That theory has been covered. But Pokemon could also be muted creatures in the future. The technology, appearance, and some pieces of technology make it all tie together. If Pokemon and Kirby are both from the Fallout universe, that's another connection to the real world. BUT IT DOESN'T STOP THERE. If Kirby and Pokemon are from the same world, that could mean that Kirby is a Pokemon. That may ALSO mean many characters of Mario are actually mutants from the fallout world. If people from Mario are mutants, that would mean that many humans went to seek habitable worlds, which could tie in Halo and Killzone. Yet another connection to the real world.
On top of that, many creatures from other games could ALSO be mutants in the extremely far future. Maybe W.o.W. is actually scientific. After all, if we can create stimpaks and energy weapons, we might as well have magic. That would mean we have many many many many other connections to similar universes.

First time i ever did LSD was the most life altering experience i ever had. Its not just seeing colors. Its seeing the world through new eyes. First time i did it i ended up getting caught in the rain waiting in a bus shelter soaking wet and freezing not sure how i was gonna get home as i watched the intense black storm clouds over head. Until i realized that i wasn't wet and it wasn't raining at all and all the puddles i had been running through had just been in my mind the whole time. Even though i could see the rain and water and even feel it. It just wasn't real and was all in my mind. I didn't even realize that i was hallucinating!!! And that's only part of the first wave of the trip because it attacks you in waves and wave number 1 is a realization of reality wave before it gets even more crazier. It all felt way too real and included all my senses in believing everything that was happening. Its a drug that makes you believe the matrix is a true story and i would never recommend it to anyone!!! However i learned a very valuable lesson from it. That our perceptions are completely chemical. And just because you can see it hear it smell it and even feel it doesn't mean that its real. The binomial distribution only works if each outcome is independent from the previous, but in the Plinko machine if the chip goes in one direction for a while, it's going to keep going in that direction (intertia, if it's been going left for 3 pegs, it's probably going to keep going left). Actually, the reason people dressed in costumes on LMaD was so Monty would pick them, as to try and stand out. Initially, all contestants were in the usual attire, suits, dresses, slacks, and the like. One person dressed funny to stand out, and it worked. People saw that, then dressed funny as well, so now (in his run) it was how well you stood out in order of getting picked.
Today, you're screened, and if your personality seems good enough to work on TV, you'll be picked. Plain-clothed people do get picked on the modern version (rare as that kind of dress is).

I think I get it. Basically the probability works because the likely hood you chose a zonk in the first place was 66% to begin with. So most likely you're on a zonk with the one you picked. After the other one that's a zonk is revealed, you increase your chance of getting the good prize because you likely picked a bad one to begin with. The odds go in your favor when the other bad prize and where it is has been eliminated from your choice.

The reason it doesn't "reset" is because it was high that you chose a zonk to begin with. That doesn't change when the other one is revealed.

I had a hard time wrapping my head around this one till I thought of the 66% being against you until the other place of a bad prize was revealed.
I still have some issues, I suppose my brain is just not oriented to think this way. I felt like it resets, but it doesn't because your odds were not equal to begin with.

It's a great play on human psychology too. We like to stick pridefully with our instincts, even though gut feelings are usually wrong.

All gambling, shows and anything having to do with winning are stacked up so you're likely to be the loser.

Humans aren't good with probability in the face of dopamine based promise, or the idea of reward. That's why gambling works.

Mostly accurate. One thing about the 'Plinko' problem, though, Newton's first law of motion. If I were to drop the chip on either extreme ends of the board, the chip would, either at the first or second peg, have to move towards the center. According to Newton's first law of motion, this 'object in uniform motion' would have a tendency to continue in the same direction at the next peg. This could mean that your best bet would be to drop the chip at one of the ends of the board.
Also, the statistics would change at the sides, because the possible outcomes are reduced. For example, if the chip was dropped at the far left, going to the left six times would be impossible because it would bounce off the side before then.

Even *IF* Jynx was supposed to be a black woman I highly doubt the person who designed her made her look that way to make fun of black people or because he hated them or anything.
It's just an exaggeration.
When drawing a caricature or cartoon you exaggerate certain features.
Just like western cartoon artists, the japanese tend to do that in their designs. Their eyes and heads are *really* big and their hair is *really* long.
So an exaggerated skin tone doesn't feel that out of place if you think about it.

Sure, today we associate that with minstrel shows and blackface characters but that doesn't mean the intent behind Jynx's design was the same.

Not to mention, when Pokemon first came out it was on the Gameboy which only allowed a limited number of colors and shades on fairly small sprites. So making her face and hands completely black was an easy way to create a strong visual contrast.

Kadabra is said to be a boy who woke up one day transformed into a Pokemon... a SECOND STAGE Pokemon. Jynx could easily fit that bill as well). But Jynx has sparked some passion among some black friends of mine because shes "racist".

Most Americans dont know the Japanese cultures at all (Well, ANY cultures, even our own) yet we claim we want to be like them. You give us a JRPG and were like "Wow, I feel just like an Asian" but if a Japanese person tries to tell us the myth of Prince Okuninushi were like "Lol no, what are you talking about?" and not even give them a chance to finish. So few of us actually RESEARCH before we claim, or we only base our studies and believes off on one source and not give any other culture a say. Humans are simple creatures, we see what we see and dont see what we dont want to see, even if there are studies, evidences and facts that fly in their faces. If a black person sees Jynx as racist, Jynx will always be racist to them and that person will ignore all evidence and even try to dispute the evidence found.

Finally, Racism will never end unless we want it to end. In a way, racism is contributed directly by the race in question. A black person is "entitled" to use the N words how they want because they are of that color or its their "heritage", but every other race, be it white or even asian, is excluded ad the black people will get mad when they hear this word, even if they themselves use it. Racism and Stereotyping is nothing more than an imprint now. All Americans are overweight, gun wielding, ignorant drunks and all white people are geeky/nerdy, sun fearing and sports inapt because thats all we show of ourselves. Few people even know Baseball was predominantly a white persons sport when it was first founded and its our fault. We teach our children about "American" History but so often, the American part is left out. Sure, we teach them about how America was founded and the Civil War and other important events, but America is much more than that, yet we neglect to tell those stories more often than not, meaning the only things we learn about other cultures is though media, and when that teacher is Teri Hatcher, Paul DelVecchio or Honey Boo Boo, we dont learn alot that is positive or factual, we learn the exact things that make us racist or stereotypical.

I'm not sure Belle had Stockholm Syndrome. In the story, the Beast does several things to earn her respect and love such as saving her life, showing her kindness and proving he isn't actually a "beast" but just misunderstood.

Not to mention she wasn't exactly being held captive in the most unpleasant of conditions. I mean, she was being kept in an enchanted castle where her every need and want was being attended to.

The reason why most captives develop Stockholm syndrome isn't because they actually like their captives or even begin to understand them, it's actually a defense mechanism triggered by the urge to survive. The subconscious logic behind it is who are your captors more likely to kill? A captive who fights them and is a problem for them or someone who supports what they are doing and has managed to endear themselves to them? The answer is simple. They are going to kill the problem captive. So, what happens without the captive realizing is their brains begin to rationalize and even sympathize with their captors cause in order to stay alive. Think of it as your brain's fail-safe to try and keep you from shooting off your mouth around the lunatics with guns so they don't shoot it off for you.

There's a different disorder for Peach. Similar to bipolar but its most noticeable difference: how frequent and severe the mood swings are. Its called borderline personality disorder. Example: bipolar disorder- depressed for  month, manic for a month. Borderline personality disorder- depressed, manic, blinding rage, and hysteria all in a few hours with the right circumstance and severe enough case. Also a big difference: bipolar disorder is a genetic disorder. And while it is more common to have borderline personality disorder if you have another mental disorder, it is more the end result of trauma, difficult childhoods, abuse, or things like getting kidnapped frequently by a giant turtle/dinosaur. Hello, and welcome to theoryception. The only YouTube comment that analyzes Game Theory *intro music* welcome to theoryception.
Now, in the old intro, the Game Theory logo has four strips of light clearly mimicking that of an XBox logo. This is further shown by the fact it turns red. However, this red shows us something more. The red on Xbox controllers has always symbolized a controller not working. However, the Game Theory logo does not have an Xbox logo, but a G and a T. This shows us that YouTube and watching this is a controller of the show, which is an entirely other episode, and that the logo is talking about GameTheory, not xbox. And we can see that the red starts to be taken up by green, showing that a controller is working. What this means is amazing. This means that Game Theory is a way to make gaming work. In repeated episodes, Game Theory has talked about ignoring game logic. This shows us that this show is making gaming work and connect. But hey! That's just a theory. A Youtube Theory. Thanks for reading.

The number of pops that this will produce once you can decide that will depend on if you're playing with it or what words it's in. This was good, but I object to one thing partially. 

Tyson wasn't really a bad fighter after 4 rounds. Especially since Mike Tyson's Punch Out came in 1987, one of Tyson's greatest years as a fighter. At that time, he could beat outbox people long after the fourth round with a similar tenacity. Refer to his fights against such fighters as Mitch Green, James Tillis, or (especially) Bonecrusher Smith.

His descent as a fighter began in 1988, when he was urged to fire his trainer Kevin Rooney. By that point, his defensive dissolved and his conditioning tapered, becoming a one trick pony with vicious punches, doing heavy lifting rather than the head and back strengthening exercises he used to do religiously. 

So, the idea that Tyson tires out after a few rounds is a fair argument to make, but not for the Mike Tyson visualized in Mike Tyson's Punch Out. It would certainly apply to the Tyson you saw fight against Holyfield and Lewis (Oh, and by the way, Tyson hardly used Peek-a-Boo like I said after the 1990s, so by the Lewis fight it was more Tyson not really being good at dodging than Lewis outsmarting him. He bobbed his head, but not like he used to - not even close). But it wouldn't apply to the Mike we see here.

Maybe the glitch at the beginning of FNV, where Doc Mitchell's head turned, wasn't an actual glitch. Maybe it's the aftermath of the courier getting shot in the head by Benny and having sustained minor brain damage! Mind=Blown.

Benny's pistol could also have misfired, and since one can load their own rounds in the game, it is quite possible, whether Benny or an underling hand-loaded the rounds, the rounds were packed with insufficient powder. This is a likely scenario because Doc Mitchel plainly states HE HAD TO DIG OUT THE BULLET. This would mean the round was highly likely a junk round (very lousy ammo in the game), and had a lower muzzle velocity. Benny is portrayed as a poser; he acts like a big shot, but he's also cheap (he weasels out of paying the Khans he hired). I also doubt Benny is much of a marksman since he, again, is a poser. He relies on his underlings to do his dirty work, and let's face it, none of them are very bright. So combined with lack of marksmanship, a highly decorative - yet mediocre - 9mm pistol that probably hasn't been properly maintained, and finally the high probability of low-grade ammo combined with poor maintenance means Courier Six could have survived a headshot fired from Maria. As for the second shot you mentioned, when did that happen? Doc Mitchel only dug out ONE bullet. As for bleeding, Vic pulled you from the ground and brought you to Doc Mitchel within minutes of being shot, increasing the odds of post-headshot survival greatly. Finally, one last aspect that probably doesn't matter, but considering the odds of junk rounds being used, deserts get cold at night, and it's quite possible a poorly crafted bullet would allow excess gasses to escape ahead of the bullet due to imperfections in the geometry combined with condensing metal as the temperature drops. Like I said, that probably doesn't make much of a difference, but it sure doesn't help.

It is literally impossible, even with a critical hit, for Maria to kill the player in the cutscene using the combat damage tables. Even at level 1, Endurance 1, no armor, and assuming Benny has maxed Guns skill (he doesn't), it doesn't do enough damage to kill even with the critical and headshot bonuses. Ok, I might have to point out a few flaws in your theory... here's four points that might be clarifying.

1. You CAN find hollow point ammo in the Mojave Wasteland. It's very uncommon, but there are merchants that sell different ammo types in the wasteland, such as the standard FMJ, to even incendiary rounds. It's just few and far between to find it. I'll bet my Pipboy 3000 somebody sells 9mm ammo variants it in the game. Benny probably only had standard (not hollow) ammo on hand when he tried to kill you, thus that why you probably survived.

2. You DID suffer memory loss in the game. (SPOILER) It is implied that you used to do Courier work, and that you were supposed to deliver a package to Benny. There was a snafu, and Benny decided to take the package for himself, I believe... Haven't played to far in the game, but that's the main thing I remember...

3. The Geneva Convention doesn't mean crap in 2281. Otherwise there wouldn't have been a full scale nuclear war in the first place. Everybody used the most devastating weapons they could find or make. They even have a gun called the Fat Man, which is a friggin portable mini-nuke gun! Definitely not friendly with the Geneva Convention. O_o

4. Benny goofed up when he tried killing you. Like you said at the end. It take two shots to kill you in the head point blank (insert movie reference here). The first would render you unconscious. After Benny shot you once, he probably would have thought you were dead, since you appeared to be, when you were actually unconscious. What if it was a perfect storm? Benny's bad aim, standard ammo instead of hollow point, and a doctor nearby to fix your head. That would be extremely lucky, but hey. After all, you ARE in Vegas!

Also... BOOM. Headshot!

.22+brain almost always equals death. It's a very valuable tool for real life hitmen because the bullet has enough force to go into the skull but, usually, not enough to escape it. So it ricochets in your head. 

I'm not going to speak on headshots with other calibre guns but in real life higher calibre bullets tend to have a lower mortality rate for body shots than lower calibre bullets. Hence why a lot of murder victims were killed with lower calibre bullets and why so many people survive a .50 calibre shot (again, talking about body shots). Bullets are said to be most dangerous when there's no escape wound.

Leafy green vegetables are the only natural source of creatine phosphate, the energy molecule white muscle tissues use. While muscle fibers are also what athletes who focus on instant power and speed like 100m dashers. This is because creatine phosphate runs out VERY quickly and we cannot store much of it.

Creatine is found in almost all muscle building energy drinks and such because white muscles are very easy to bulk up as they can tear themselves very quickly without needing oxygen.
So yes, if you feed someone a green veggie sludge and compare their energy output with someone who ate a bowel of oatmeal initially the green sludge will provide a greater energy boost; however, in the long run the complex sugars in the oatmeal will give you a longer release of total caloric energy.

Fatal flaw in the argument MatPat (big fan of this channel by the way.  You're making an awfully large assumption that what we're witnessing with the suit is something that is affecting air pressure and not simply oxygen supply.  Remember about 30 seconds before Shepherd is thrusted in that vacuum of space?  The entire cockpit was ripped open and exposed to space, but its pressure remained because of Mass Effect fields, however their simply was no oxygen.  Thats why Joker needed to wear a oxygen mask and not a space suit.  

So when we see Shepherd's suit leaking some sort of gas, I feel we could safely assume that the air supply was ruptured, and that the integrity of the suit remained intact because of Mass Effect fields.  I mean, if you read the codex, EVERYTHING in the game relies on these Mass Effect fields, even the technology that allows Shepherds guns to stick to his back.  So, given the widespread use of Mass Effect fields in literally, everything, we could reasonably assume the integrity of the suit did not break, but he did lose his oxygen.  So he didn't die from exposure to the vacuum of space but in fact, it was simply, suffocation.  Thanks for reading!

After watching this (and having played through the Mass Effect Trilogy + all DLC), I think I might have another theory.  WARNING: IF YOU HAVEN'T PLAYED THROUGH THESE GAMES, THERE MIGHT BE SPOILERS.

As stated in this video, Miranda did basicly say Shepard may be beyond revival.  As for Shepard not following the path of either Paragon or Renegade, since those descisions (including a Paragon/Renagade bonus that reflects said descisions) are transfered through the games via the save game import systems, I'm proposing that the Lazarus Project actually managed to bring Shepard back as either an AI or a super-advanced VI.

As the Assassin's Creed Animus episode stated, we're already at the point where we can store memories as some sort of file via extraction from the DNA or something (I honestly don't remember, it was that long ago).  Considering most of the experiences Shepard has lived through have left some form of imprint on him/her AND that Project Lazarus had virtuqally unlimited resources thanks to the backing of the Illusive Man, I'd say it's very safe to assume that the scientist who pieced Shepard back together would have had the most advanced version of the DNA extraction/memory imprint scanner.

That said, it is entirely possible that they found the right number of memories to compile an AI or VI construct with enough knowledge of Shepard (Paragon/Renegade descisions, military feats, childhood scarring, relationships, etc) and the programming installed to believe it's Shepard.  Now, I know it sounds completely insane, but it has happened in the Mass Effect games on a different occassion (ME3 to be precise).  If you go to Huerta Memorial Hospital at a certain point in game, you run across a woman arguing with one of the desk attendants about the name.  During the argument , the woman states that the man who essentially created the hospital, named Huerta, isn't dead; in response, the desk attendant states that the medical records stated that he was in fact dead and his body was running about with a VI in his head that thought he was Huerta.  That means that the Technology is already in the hands of medical professionsals.  Now, once again considering the mass resources being pooled into Lazarus via the Illusive Man, it is again possible for the Cerberus cell to have access to this technology two years before anyone else (two years being a rough estimate between the events of ME3 and the start of Project Lazarus).  So, since Huerta was made into a VI construct in a body, the most likely senario (in my opinion at least) is that Shepard is in fact and Unshackled AI inside a dead body; I'm ruling out the VI possibility because the Illusive Man needed Shepard to be unbarred by restrictions in terms of making all kinds of descisions (including those based in ethics) and the fact the Huerta died on the Citadel (a place that has an artificial atmosphere (hence, not much brain decay), unlike Shepard (who died in the vaccum of space, so brain mush = more memory and CPU required).

MatPat, I really hate to say it, considering the great amount of work you put into it, but, your theory on CloneShep has a hole in it, thanks to the Mass Effect 3: Citadel DLC.  In it, Shepard runs into a clone, but, from what I could gather from it's tone and the way it treats members of your crew, it has none of Shepard's memories (which seem to be fully in tact).  Also, if Shepard decides to ask the clone "how many of you are there", it responds saying that it was the only one.  It might have been lying, too; I'd have to go back and see if Miranda had anything to say about the CloneShep.  For right now, I'd say that the evidence I found just may be a bit more plausible the CloneShep armies theory.

Again, just my opinion.

I need to state 2 big flaws in this review..., first, in the beginning you say that he was rebuilt 2 years after he died, but it was a process that took 2 years to complete...
The second is that you assume that in the ME timeline a snapped oxigen cable would compromise the suits internal pressure... I'dd have to state that you are wrong in this... In the game you see Mirande (and even Liara and other characters too) not wearing any real space suits, but are still able to walk around in almost 0G environments... So it is safe to "assume" that in that time they have technology that allows you to stay pressurized normally without an actual space suit, making shepard's suit a mere armor, less space protection... And this is, afaik, possible, since the use of Meta-materials can make objects invisible (even today, though only basic shapes) and various other uses, it could defy 0G pressure, keeping your body intact... I have to agree he wouldn't be able to be the old Shepard due to the brain damage because of the lack of oxigen before dying, but his body could have been restored almost completely (even the brain, but he would be an empty living husk)... In Mass Effect 3 Citadel DLC it is stated that Shepard did have a clone made just in case, but he was the real deal, and I know this intel was not available at the making of this video, but it is not something to be dismissed...
Conclusion: the use of stem-cells, advanced technology, and the technology to survice on no-oxigen and 0G environments through, what I think is, meta materials could mean that Shepard's body was most likely cryogenically stored, inflicting a lot of damage, but keeping the body rather preserved in the process, making it able to be rebuilt the way it was... The only impossible part is that he did not have any brain damage whatsoever...

You look around a little, or at least focus on different parts of their face. But if someone has heard of the whole "liars make less eye contact" thing (which many, many people have), they'll often actually focus on making more eye contact in an attempt to seem more truthful. It's important to note that these are not always indicators of lying.  Some Asian cultures do not make eye contact if there is a status difference between the parties as it can be interpreted as the lower class challenging the higher class.  Too much or too little eye contact can be a sign of lying (either you're trying hard to sell it or trying hard not to detect it).  Ideally, it's best to have a baseline and compare it against the baseline (which is also what the lie detector machine does).  You'll also note it's easier to detect lies in the interviews than at the end, because MatPat is just reading a prepared statement vs. being able to read the genuine surprise in a line of questioning.  Other, more advanced methods are used to identify prepared lies, such as having you recite events in a different order or backwards to combat the order you rehearsed them in.

Pinocchio saying "My nose will grow now" is not a paradox, never in the original children's novel "The adventures of Pinocchio" was there ever stated that Pinocchio's nose can ONLY grow by him lying. If the road is slippery, that doesn't necessarily mean that it has been raining, heck, someone could've just picked up a garden hose and start firing away, but as soon as it's raining, that DOES mean that the road is wet (as long as there's no cover over the road, of course, but that aside..). This actually leaves out a huge caveat to the Geneva conventions. A lot of the examples you used, and especially in your montage there's a problem:
Some of those structures are being illegally used by enemy combatants. They may be religious or national landmarks, but they ARE aiding the war effort. The second the enemy uses a building for military efforts outside of the building's intention (placing artillery on the roof of a hospital, shooting from the windows of a church or belltower, etc) they lose their "protected" status.

Furthermore, a lot of people ignore some very important words very early into the Geneva convention, which is that the Conventions apply to a signatory nation even if the opposing nation is not a signatory, but ONLY IF THE OPPOSING NATION "accepts and applies the provisions" of the Conventions. So, in the early example where they use gas against that Warlord, that would actually be a pretty gray area, legally speaking. Because by using the gas himself, he had already shown that he DOESN'T accept and apply the provisions of the conventions, as he had already broken them.

Now, MORALLY speaking, you may be right. But, legally speaking, many of these things are actually pretty legally defensible, especially for permanent members of the UN. One of the BIGGEST no-no's however, is the use of explosive traps. Legally, someone HAS to keep an eye on any modern explosive booby traps, to prevent situations like exist in Vietnam and even Europe today, where there are live buried explosives just lying around and no way of knowing where each individual one is. So, every time you lay down a Claymore mine in some doorway and then walk away without keeping an eye on it, you're committing a war crime.

You're a bit wrong on labeling many of these actions as war crimes. When fighting a war, you're allowed to fight back. If you're taking fire from a house or a mosque/church/cemetery/some dudes house/ect, you fire back. Bunch of troops in the statue of liberty? Expect lady liberty to see a bit of damage while you root them out. No one has been convicted of a war crime for property damage while engaging troops using said property. Hell, I don't think anyone's bothered to even accuse, let alone try, someone for that.

Now, the intentional targeting of non-combatants is a whole new can of worms. Blowing up someones house because you got a big gun and want to see something go boom? That fits in. Much of what you showed however was relevant collateral damage in combat.

As for the interrogations, it's pretty hard to try someone for such things without evidence that they were there. Should they fail and leave conclusive evidence that what his nuts popped open some chemical weapons, there'd be pretty big issues. By leaving no witnesses, they're pretty free to fudge the reports. Right? No. But deniability makes it very difficult to bring charges against anyone. If you can't prove the SAS sent people in, you can't pin what happened on the SAS, and no one in the SAS gets hung out to dry. "War crimes" only matter if you get caught, and if someone can make the crimes stick, which is pretty hard to do if you didn't just win the war. Unfortunately, the winners aren't all for trying their own soldiers for war crimes.

My great grandmother is dutch and I was raised knowing about Zwarte Piet. The version of the story that I know says that the Original Zwarte Piet was from the Spanish moors and had dark skin. When Sinter Klaas moved to Spain, he met this kind, friendly, child loving man and they became friends. (I remember once being told that Sinter Klaas moved into PIET'S house, not the other way around) When Sinter Klaas wanted to give gifts to children, he borrowed Piet's boat to travel to them. The Sint was an old man, and couldn't get to all the places he wanted to, but Piet was young and quite the acrobat, so he COULD get into things like Chimneys!
When kids misbehaved, Piet took them to Spain to give their parents a break, and try to teach the children better ways. Several children DID never return, but it was because they liked it better (some felt guilty for how they behaved too). When Zwarte Piet got too old to climb into chimneys anymore, some of the boys did it in his place, with their skin being blackened by the soot (their lips are red from rubbing soot off because it gets in their mouths) this "turns them into" Zwarte Piets themselves, which is why there can be more than one. The girls stay in Spain and take care of the house until Piet and Sinter Klaas return.
Tell me, does this sound racist to you? This may not be a common tale, and just my family version, but I don't associate it with racism. I associate it with friendship, helping others and teaching children respect. That's who Zwarte Piet is to me!

The gun-blade does have a functional gun. If you press X at the right point in his swing you do extra damage coupled with a gun sound and mini explosion. Is that not the gun-blade firing?

EDIT: Yes and no... The gun blade doesn't actually fire bullets or projectiles at all. Pressing R1 at the right time, simply discharges (or fires) the weapon, causing a chainsaw-like effect through vibrations in the blade. So actually, it's more like an electric meat carver lol... The only difference is, instead of using electricity to oscillate the blade, it uses the kinetic energy generated by the force of the discharge. This triggered discharge is the mini explosion we see and _technically_ is the gun-blade "firing"...

i hate how nobody ever tries to understand how the gunblades from final fantasy 8 work.  they are not guns with a massive blade attached they are blades with an accelerator attached to improve cutting power with out requiring the wielder to be strong enough to put the equivalent force into the swing.  the gunblades are literally a blade on a pivot that uses the force of the "bullet" to increase swing speed and force which is why pulling the trigger too early or too late will not cause an increase in damage, because the extra force will either change the angle of the blade before the cut has started or the force of the shot will cause the handle to shift rather then the blade if there is not enough momentum after the cut has been started and the overall force of the swing was already absorbed by the target.

If you take a "field trip" to the Naval Aviation Museum here in Florida, you can go into a simulator. And the instructors tell you how to fly and what not, but if you ask to do tricks, they tell you how to do a "barrel roll." I told the person controlling mine that it was an aileron roll, and he got really embarrassed. Then he just said "Well, so, uh, these are the ailerons on the plane." It sounds ungrateful, but don't you think they should teach people the right terms?

Having done some more research, the barrel roll, wasn't meant for the plane being attacked, but the attack himself. When done properly, the maneuver will put your aircraft back on the 6 o'clock of it's opponent, allowing him to continue firing. Used mostly by faster aircraft who are attacking slower aircraft.
