This episode fails to account for the G-Diffuser, a flight support system that uses [Anti]Gravity. There is no "Tactical Egg" when your "aircraft" uses gravity itself to move.

While the term "Barrel Roll" is incorrect, the concept of a spinning hypergravity field [In order to have Anti-gravity, you have to have hypergravity somewhere.] makes for an INCREDIBLY powerful deflection system. All that would be needed to send plasma or laser bolts spinning away from the craft by tight rolling would be that field moving along the wings...
And that's actually what the wings on the Arwings do- they disperse the hypergravity generated by the G-diffusers. This is why if your wings are blown off in say Sector X, which is a massive interplanetary nebula, you get a downward motion: The G-Diffuser can no longer support the gravitational fields needed to keep a stable "flight".

The somersault is also more similar to a Kulbit, which IS a near perfect circle [or rather the plane performing a backflip] (its something an F-22 Raptor or other "supermaneverable fighter" would do if you got that close on it's 6. It'd flip itself over and wind up behind you.)... the point of the maneuver is for foes to pass it, allowing for a counterattack.

Although I can't argue with Peppy's lack of basic aviator diction, the argument about physics is null as soon as you try to compare a terrestrial fighter jet with a multifaceted spacecraft/aircraft that clearly doesn't abide by the laws of nature that our technology is capable of in or outside of space. And speaking of space, no gravity and no air drag = no loss of velocity. Which explains how they are able to move so freely off of other planets.

But as for when they're near planet surfaces--you point out yourself that the airwing's Loop maneuver is a perfect circle, which is impossible...unless they don't abide by the planet's gravity. Not to mention (but I'll mention it anyway) the only jets on the airwing are the thrusters located on the back, yet the aircrafts are able to stay suspended like a helicopter even when they slow down to crawling speeds. I think it's safe to assume they have their own gravity fields. Not like it's an original concept in sci-fi.

I know this video is years old, and Matpat's game is making entertainment out of these fun applications of game logic to real life, BUT...
The explanation for almost every qualm in this video is the science-fiction properties of the Arwing.  The G-diffusor explains their aerobatics ignoring the law of gravity, and therefore the principal of exchanging kinetic and potential energy.  Since gravity isn't acting on the Arwing, it doesn't slow down upon ascent, and doesn't suffer the disadvantages that real world aircraft do when doing the same maneuver.  And while an aileron roll is impractical in real life, obviously the Arwing's super crazy sci-fi force field is what makes it an effective maneuver for the Star Fox team.

While these are pretty much "get out of jail free" sci-fi mumbo jumbo made up by the developers to explain these things away, they're far to obvious to keep from at least mentioning!

Jynx is based off of a yokai, Yama-uba. One kinda similar to Froslass' Yuki-onna. They both live in snowy mountains to capture and eat wandering humans. The wiki on Yama-uba even addresses this comparison, stating where Jynx's appearance likely comes from.

It could be possible that Jynx was based off of Black Face, however it doesn't necessarily mean that, from Game Freak's perspective, that Jynx was offensive. It's a problem in the U.S. because African Americans were persecuted and oppressed for over three centuries in America and enslaved for near nearly 2 of those centuries. To the extent of my knowledge, Japan never kept African slaves and the Japanese never had problems with African persecution(They had problems with Chinese oppression, but that is for another discussion). Since they were never persecuted by that ethnic group to begin with, I therefore do not think Game Freak was intentionally making in Jynx with the attention of offending anyone of African origins.

That song is accurate...well kind of. The proper definition of racist is a person who believes that a particular race is superior to another. So I don't think everyone is racist, but I think a lot of people say things or think things of a person based on race (even if on accident). Sometimes accidentally thinking something of someone based on their race is not being racist and it doesn't make you a horrible person. As long as you correct yourself after thinking this thing, then I think it's ok. That's the way I see it at least.

Okay, Jynx does admittedly have a design that I can't very well defend as being non-racist (although if it is based on Ganguro, which it still could very well be, then it probably isn't racist--the fad wasn't a racist fad). However, the Pokemon games and anime have gone far out of their way to include characters of many different races, languages, and cultures without prejudice against them. Many of the messages within the games are ones of equality and tolerance of different ideas. The games even sometimes mock closed-minded people. If anything, I'd say there's a lot more going against the idea that Pokemon has racist ideas in it than there is going for it.

This isn't really relevant to the video 100%, but I need to get this off my chest. I'm Buddhist, so naturally I'd wear a "swastika" necklace or bracelet. The "swastika" is actually a Buddhist symbol for good luck, but that is apparently a little known fact. Since I can't get tattoos until I'm 18 in my state, I would get temporarily inked with Buddhist symbols. One of which being the "swastika." Swastika is actually the German term for it. If you asked me to spell the original, Chinese/Indian name for it, I couldn't. The difference between a swastika and the Buddhist symbol is the direction they face. I got sent to the principal's office because the teacher said I was "representing neonazi symbolism," which is also not grounds for punishment to begin with. Freedom of Worship. It just bugs me that people don't know the difference between a Swastika, and the Buddhist symbol that I wear. I get judgmental looks all the time for it.

Wrong again, Cronos is the son of Gaia, the Earth, and Uranus, the sky. The myth said that in the beginning, there was "Chaos", which in Ancient Greek is a void. From that void, Gaia was born, and things such as night and Eros were born too. In Euripides' version Uranus, Tartarus and Oceanus were born then too, but in more common version, it is Gaia who give birth to those three, and she mated with Uranus, due to Eros, which give birth to Cronos. Then they have the cyclops and the 100-handers.

The first two generation of gods, Gaia's and her son's, make up most of the universe, supposedly. Cronos just took over. But in some version, he is responsible for creating a prototype of human...

The story of Dracula as written Bran Stoker is actually based on the stories of Elizabeth Bathory. Bran Stoker knew very little about Vlad Tepes, most scholars believe that he read a short synopsis of his life in a local Romanian church, where he got the name “Dracula” and the place where his villain would reside. Bathory is where most of the lore around blood, temper, death and not aging come from. Though Vlad did kill many, and was brutal, Bathory killed children, young woman and slaves (usually wanting virgins). She would take their blood so that she could drink and bath in it, to keep herself young and beautiful.

Actually, it's shown that pokemon can just resist to fight. But, they trust their trainer. There is even a friendship stat in the games, that will go higher with fighting.

Actually, if the 1st Pokémon movie is to go by, pokemon battles aren't really "battles", instead they're probably meant to be sparring matches, which is generally a practice fight where you don't (ordinarily) beat the holy hell out of each other to the point of being hospitalized but, instead, usually to being exhausted.

In cricket, if the bowler (the equivalent of the pitcher), hits one of the wickets (the stick things) with the ball, the batter is out. The batter didn't hold the bat in the right area and the ball hit a wicket. It is a "Fail" because he didn't hold the bat in the right area and he's a professional player.

If youre gonna pull from the anime, then there are SEVERAL examples of pokemon being able to disobey their trainers commands and can resist being put inside a pokeball even when captured. So even though they are suffering burns and paralysis, the ones that stay like ash's pikachu, are doing it and staying all because they want to. Pokemon love to fight each other and there is a genuine friendship mechanic. Its like going hunting with your retriever.

The Pokemon games reward the player for being nice and loving their Pokemon. The only exception being the TM Frustration, but the Return TM cancels that out. Many monsters only evolve at max happiness. In X and Y, there's the Pokemon Amie mini game, where the player pets, feeds, and plays with their monsters, which causes them to be stronger in battle. It's a huge contrast to dog fights, where the dogs are abused to become stronger.

In X and Y, there's a character (I think it's a gym leader, not sure) who asks before you battle- Why do we battle using our Pokemon. After you win, he says that battling side-by-side with your Pokemon strengthens the bond of friendship between a trainer and his/her Pokemon. The entire game may be structured around violence, but the core theme of Pokemon is friendship.

Considering the fact that all these horrible things done in fights involving flame throwers, high pressure water cannons, lightning, etc. are completely natural to the development of the Pokemon I think it's a fair assumption they were literally born to fight. Note that all moves learned by Pokemon over time are learned naturally excluding TM's and HM's which are natural moves that can simply be taught. If you think about it, the only Pokemon that were even ever engineered by humans were Mewtwo, Ditto and Genesect which were all created by moderately evil organizations in attempt to create the most powerful Pokemon in the world. Also it's kind of bizarre to call Pokemon "defenseless animals" when they are the entire base of that universe. Take Arceus, the literal God of Pokemon and compare him to a Magikarp. They are both considered Pokemon and it's kind of insane to call them both defenseless when one created the universe with a thought and the other grows into a giant fucking water dragon that shoots laser beams from it's mouth.

I like to think of the Pokémon games sort of like GTA. What you're doing in this virtual scenario might not be in any way moral, but that doesn't necessarily mean you playing the game means you support such ideas. Some people argue against this but just because you play something like GTA or Saint's Row where your "protagonist" does almost nothing but shoot people, rob stores, and jack cars to get their missions complete, doesn't mean you actually want to or wish you could do these things in real life. We play Pokémon for the satisfaction of completing a virtual collection of adorable animals that also double as badass battle drones. It doesn't mean we're going to turn around and do the same thing with our own pets. It just means we found a fun, fictional scenario we liked as children and still like now. As kids, most of us probably never even connected the Pokémon world to any sort of abuse or real violence. They were nifty things to collect and that's all there was to it.

There's one major point that MatPat completely failed to address in this episode. Pokémon, in both the games and the anime, have been shown to have intelligence. In the anime, Pokémon have been shown to have conversations with each other. Meowth (which learned to fully speak whatever language the anime is dubbed in) even translates for Pokémon in some episodes. Similarly, in the games, N will actually talk to your Pokémon and relay what they said back to you. Turns out, Pokémon enjoy spending time with their trainers and are willing to do battle for them. Now, it could be argued that the starter Pokémon have been conditioned to obey humans, possibly through techniques that would be called animal cruelty in this world, and the early game Pokémons (Caterpie, Patrat et cetera) are too weak to resist the whims of their trainers. But this logic falls apart when you start catching a wild Onix or Gyarados. Those fully grown beasties are more than able to just bugger off, or even attack the trainer that beat them up, shoved them into a tiny ball and took them far away from their natural habitat. Instead, they fight for you from the get-go. The only time a Pokémon will willingly disobey their trainer is if they were traded and said trainer hasn't shown that they're worthy of such a powerful monster (worth being represented by badges).

When you consider this, a Pokémon battle turns from a cockfight into a boxing match; two willing competitors battling it out to see who is the most superior in pugilism.

A lot of things in the anime actually explain a lot of stuff, including the fact that Pokémon WANT to get caught by trainers. The only reason they resist is to test the strength of the trainer to see if they're worthy enough. 
It's also actually against the Pokémon League rules to go out and randomly injure wild Pokémon in battle without the intent of capturing it. The only reason you can do it in game is cause... well, how else you going to get those experience points?

There are also two other rules that should of been mentioned in this video. One of them being that a trainer can't force a Pokémon to fight. In fact, if a Pokémon refuses to fight for you in battle, then that is decleared as a loss(of battle or round). 
The other thing is that there is a limit to the amount of harm a Pokémon is allowed to take, and the match is usually stopped should this limit be reached. Also, they don't actually become unconscious, but faint and then come to a few seconds later. The reason you can use HM moves outside of battle in game, even when they've been beaten in a battle, is because they can still move around and uses attacks/moves. The Pokémon League rules do not permit them to battle due to not being in a certain level of health however, so that's the game doesn't allow you to use them anymore in battle until you've healed them up.

...Just a few things I thought I'd mention. I don't usually do this sort of thing, but I was bored so... meh. XP

While I like the theory, it does collapse when looked upon with a bit more thought.

1. Pokemon are capable of direct communication, especially in the case of psychic types. They are also as intelligent as people.

Good reasons of augmentations:
1) You can have any part you lost back.
2) It enchances EVERYTHING. You run faster, you are more quiet, you are stronger....better everything!
3) Augmentation repair jobs = more people employed = $$$.

Now Bad Ones:
1) Killswitch. Imagine if a goverment had power to switch off anybody who has augmentations. Your leg would be off, your hand would be off and maybe even brain!
2) Forcing people to have the augmentations.
3) Somebody controlled you.

Here's my vision of it:

Imagine that you were in an acident, and lost your eye, leg and an arm. But now, there are augmentations. You can agree to use them, and thereby regain your body parts. Even better, if you lose them again, you can have them build back! Even better if you know how to do it yourself! Now, you are faster, stronger and efficiant than any other human. You think fast as a calculator, you are strong as a strongest man there ever would exist, and can run for hours and hours without being tired! Cool, eh? Doesn't it sound like a good future? For people to be just....better?

However, not everything is so happy. For instance, what if you had a killswitch inside you? You could die anytime if goverment or someone in high place want you to. Also, what if people were forced to have an augmentations? Even children? That is sick! Pathological, inhumane! You could even be controlled by someone! Imagine whole Human Race controlled by one man....

Therefore, I would have an augmentation. It would just upgrade me and I love the idea of being mechanical cyborg than can survive his hand chopped off. However, the augmentations should be limited. So, no killswitches or forcing people to do it - just optional. Make also humans have their free will - no enslaving. So, if there was ever a revolution I would be not sure if to join it or not. I want to be augmented, and I want for it to progess but if that would happen and they won there wouldn't be augmentations anymore. So, I would stay neutral to it. Augs are important, they are future but optional.

Superhuman strength to punch through a wall would be impossible no matter how advanced augmentation technology will become.  No matter how powerful your mechanical arm may be, it's still attached to squishy human flesh. If you tried to lift a car, your flesh is going to give away instead of the thousands of pounds of steel.

Now advanced eyes and other sensory organs would indeed be a benefit, as would replacing any parts damaged or destroyed in an accident. It is also theoretically possible to insert technology into us to make us smarter.

One aspect of this often over-looked is how this will effect insurance, business plans, and retirement. First of all, it's doubtful if any of these procedures would be covered by insurance as they would be classified as non-important vanity augmentation, similar to how lasik eye surgery and cosmetic surgery are classified as such, and thus not typically covered by insurance.

Then you also have to consider that technology to make us live longer would effect retirement. Companies would have to change their retirement plans drastically if their employees are going to be living decades, maybe even centuries longer than others. How would annual raises be done?  It's fine for someone working for 20 to 30 years, but after enough time passes, an anual percentage raise for an employee who's been working for WAAAY longer is going to cost the company far more every year.

I don't see why everyone has this fear of a killswitch or being "hacked". just make your cyber-prosthesis analogue. Not everything has to be networked, I don't see why everyone thinks everything must be connected to the Internet.
My idea is, I have a HR style arm, said arm is Faraday-cage style shielded, so no signals can pass through my arm. There is no transmitter or receiver in my arm, BUT there is a small SD style slot.
So, if there is a new upgrade for the software of my arm, I can go down, buy the new chip (which has been tested and approved to prevent against dodgy stuff) and swap it out for the one already in my arm. Therefore, I have full control of what goes in and goes out.
And logically, such software couldn't hi-jack my arm, as all it would do is basic individual tasks like regulating grip strength etc. So, if my arm went whacko for whatever reason, I just turn it off from my brain, or you could even install a physical killswitch just above where the arm is attached to the shoulder (so the switch is on human flesh and the arm couldn't move away) which is uncovered with a little sliding door using your brain.
And, really, you wouldn't even need software in a robo-arm, everything could just be done by hardware, as it's not like you're gonna plug in a keyboard, play a video game or print a document off it, so you don't need any system software, everything the arm is doing is physical.
My only worry is the social/ethical issues and the fact that electronics are massively unreliable compared to mechanical systems, so your arm could just break and would wear down over time quicker than biological parts, plus it would be difficult to maintain electronics within the human body. And what about if your arm model gets discontinued? :D

Many good reasons but... In Deus Ex, we also get a price for the augs (Yes, thats what the Humans call them), which was somewhere around a million dollars for something simple like an arm or leg. There was also a few cases where the auggers body rejected the stuff.

While Im firmly on the Human side, I do believe these augs could be helpful for the disabled or impaired. If blowing a mil could repair my back enough so I can walk normally, I'd blow that money instantly just to live normally.

But, theres also the side effects of this stuff. As I mentioned, there were a few cases in the game were the auggers body rejected the equipment. I dont remember if the effects were given but just that alone makes me wonder, could side effects exist? I mean, if you blow that mil on a new leg and your body rejects it, you lost a perfectly good Human leg. And what about possible infections? While many countries have sanitation laws, not all countries have them or the ones that do might not follow them to the letter. Finally, what about rehab? For many cases today, people spend months in rehab just to learn how to use their new prosthetic and most times are on so many pain killers and other drugs, you have to wonder about their immune systems health.

True story, my mother went in for surgery to help her back about 10 years ago. She was fine for the first few days but on the day she was to return home, she died. The cause was never clear but I remember the last day I saw her, she was taking 13 pills at once. I know there were at least three pain killers among them, which is dangerous in its own right, mixed in with other pills I couldnt recognize and those could adversely react with whatever else she was taking. Doctors make mistakes, their only human like us and no amount of augs will change that. If Deus Ex showed me anything, its that getting these augs are painful, painful enough to require months, if not years of rehab and enough medicine to make the local dealer rich enough to retire to Bermuda.

In all honesty the whole humanity argument for being against augmentation is a subjective one that varies per person. In all sense it goes back to how we define humanity. What really makes us a human? Our limbs, skin, face, our physical body? Or is what makes us human our mind? It is these two conflicting view that yield this human augmentation argument in that some believe the physical body is just as important to being human as the mind while others say it is only the mind that makes a person who he is and the physical body is irrelevant. While I like staying neutral in arguments I must put my opinion, once again, opinion, down. In all honesty when it comes down to it what makes us who we are is our personality. We are friends with people not for their physical traits...... most of the time...... but for the personality they possess. If their previous body was destroyed and plastic surgery made them completely different you would tell him or her "Hey, no matter what you are still you". The only time a  full loss of what makes someone a person can occur if the loss of the mind occurs, which is essentially death. The purity of humans is an argument that is not based on scientific terms but more along the lines of an emotional term. What people feel about the issue in general has no backing on either side other than what we can assume with information given. The entire argument for and against augmentation is in a way irrelevant so the best way to put it in a neutral place is this. Give people the option, if you hate it then don't bother with it, and try not the be racist. Its like getting plastic surgery or a piercing in a way. People disapprove it but the option is still there.

I'm sure someone else in the comments already pointed this out, but there is a major flaw in this theory: Sephiroths sword.
Sephiroths odachi is called Masamune and Masamune is often used in japanese fiction as either the name of a a single blade or a set of blades made by a bladesmith with the same name. These "cursed" blades are said to only be fit for calm and peaceful warriors, seen in their inability to draw blood, while their counterpiece, the Muramasa is fit for violent and aggressive warriors and sheds a ridiculous amount of blood.

So the reason why they didn't add blood there (or during any of Sephiroths fights in Crisis Core and Advent Children) while doing it during other fights is, that it's actually a design choice most japanese would understand while we generally don't because we're lacking cultural information.
She Shinra building is full of blood because corpses left behind by Masamune can still lose blood afterwards, it's just that the blade itself never causes instant bleeding while cutting which is also why there's never a drop of blood on a Masamune blade (not only in FF7 but in other games, anime and live action movies as well).

The rest of the theory however is still pretty interesting and amusing xD

Great, interesting, well thought out theory. I just happened to play through that part a few days ago, so I have a few more details to add:
#1: Immediately after the stabbing scene, Aeris collapses into Cloud's arms, either dead or unconscious. Cloud seems to immediately realize she's dead, and Sephiroth tells him so, as well. But then there's a boss fight, which lasts several minutes probably. 
#2: After the battle, Cloud and his other two party members have sat Aeris up, and they say their goodbyes one at a time. Several of the characters touch Aeris' body- it would likely be icy to touch by now if she was dead (climate of the area is pretty much north pole), and if she was still breathing at this point it'd probably be visible or felt by people getting so close to and handling her. 
#3: The party members have been knocked out many times by this point, usually by things much worse than a non-mortal stabbing (blown up with fire, lasers, stomped by giant beasts, etc.) and the other members seem aware that they can use some Phoenix Down to wake them up. They would likely have done this if there were signs of life.
Now all three of those points can be shot by one final thing: Sephiroth is actively manipulating Cloud and influencing his thoughts and perception of reality at this time. Maybe, after Sephiroth bailed, he realized his strike didn't do the job, so he used his mind-control over Cloud to make him perceive Aeris as being dead? And guided him to then drown her? Personally I think showing a stabbing in gruesome detail was not done because they wanted the tragedy and feelings to come from emotions, not the shock factor of gore. She may be an Ancient, but Sephiroth is Sephiroth- a stab from him is much different than a stab from anyone else, especially if he's falling out of the sky when he strikes xD

In the game's prequel novel, "Bioshock: Rapture," it's stated that the tunnels connecting Rapture's various buildings were constructed through a process of submolecular bonding, by combining glass and metal at the submolecular level. In addition, Andrew Ryan himself states in an Audio Diary in Burial at Sea that the city's support pillars were constructed on a lunette ring and made out aluminum, which is buoyant.

The series definitely goes out of its way to portray Ryan as a man very much ahead of his time, even with the technology and science being available at the time of Rapture's construction.

It also is planned to run off geothermal energy using the seabed. It's called the Ocean Spiral project and the first city is supposed to be finished by the 2030s, although if you ask me that seems pretty soon in terms of building a massive underwater city. If you look at an actual translation of the game it is actually geared towards teenagers. Lots of deaths and adult themes (like porky and his brother being abused) are omitted from the English version as well several other things. The runway five basically being the blues brothers. The hospital crosses were also removed to avoid being sued by red cross. Also the design of the cultists hats were changed because the developers didn't want them to look like the KKK. Oh another fun fact is that when it was localized in the us many enemy names were changed. For example duck became mad duck in English because it appealed to younger kids. Also Mr satruns language in Japan was one of the devs kids hand writing. Anyway this video is so old no one will probably see this but if you somehow do thanks for reading through this mess of text.

The Trojan is actually quite shitty compared to what our technology could achieve. A servomotor-assisted suit with armoring made of CNT/graphene on a dense (as in iridium, wolfram, depleted uranium) metal mesh and multi-layered trauma packs consisting of liquid armor and ceramics would be infinitely more resistant to any damage than this trojan suit. The only limitation would be the power supply, but that can be solved by either using graphene supercapacitors or exchaning servomotors for pneumatic muscles, electroactive polymers, CNT muscles or anything else. Im sorry, but Duke Nukem will always be the best. I loved Duke Nukem Forever and it will always be in my top ten best games of all time. Why? Because it was different. People complain about Duke having regen health and only carrying four guns (PC better) and yet, these same people think Call of Duty is amazing? I know what some say: "Well, Duke wasn't like that back then." Yeah, back then games were A LOT different. It was okay to carry hundreds of guns because people didn't care about the story. They only cared about the action. The plots and stories of games back then were simple and maybe only one objective: "Save the princess or save the world. Maybe both." Not much else to it.

Now today, gamers want complicated heroes with bitter sweet complicated stories that would take 3 hours just to get through the opening cutscene. Problem is, is that Duke is simple. Too simple. It was never really the game play or the graphic because I have seen games with simple game play and graphics get high schools all because the story was deep.

Another problem is gamers don't know what they want anymore. Even in the game theories on this channel show that gamers want more of the same rather than something different. A lot more intense than photographic.

So, I here I was, just minding my own business watching a playlist of Game Theories, when it hits me . . . You, MatPat, are a fantastic speaker. You introduce your topic with an attention getter and give a preview of your main points. Then you present your main points using credible sources, all the while keeping your audience interested with funny pictures and jokes. When you have made your points, you give a creative closing that helps us to remember what the purpose of your video is. You, sir, are awesome and I am a jealous college student with a speech due next week.

I learned about the Aztecs in school, and apparently they played this game called "Pok-to-pok" and the losing team would be sacrificed.  The game was played with a huge stone twice the weight of the average bowling ball, and you had to get the ball through a hoop about six feet in the air. Sounds tough?  Well, you can't use your hands, so Aztecs were hardcore as ever back then.  I also learned that the victims were sometimes painted blue (Earthbound much?) And were tied to tables atop a pyramid to humiliate them further before they died, because why not?  There's no crying in Pok-to-pok.

Just a note: 
Languages were switched up at the Tower of Babel not only as a guard against humanity's hubris, that's just the beginning. God told humanity to "fill the earth" but they weren't scattering, they were staying all in the same place. Creating multiple languages gave them reason to split up, since they could no longer communicate. 

More importantly, we go back to "Nimrod" the guy organizing the build. He was referred to as a "hunter of men", who desired to "make men like bricks". In other words, Nimrod gathered followers (a hunter of men) and wanted total uniformity in his followers eliminating their individuality (making them like bricks). In short, Nimrod was the world's first dictator. 

Nimrod encouraged humanity to build the tower in direct rebellion - specifically because they were told to scatter and fill the earth. Nimrod didn't want to scatter, he wanted to keep all of humanity in one place where he could rule over them.

It is a phenomenon of human nature that man will do things in a group that he would never do alone, and that is exactly what we see happening in the building of the tower. Nimrod gathers the people, and garners a mob mentality that (as history has shown time and again) can get people to do darn near anything. 

God, recognizing that humanity is growing increasingly wicked under Nimrod's rule. God comments that if humanity has already begun building "a tower that reaches heaven" (an attempt to be equal with God), then "nothing they plan will be impossible". This doesn't mean that humanity could actually overthrow God, but that if they've already constructed this tower in defiance to God, then there is no evil that would be "beyond them". Remember what God did the last time humanity grew "too evil"? . . . He killed everyone with a flood. 

If one reads in the Hebrew, the connotation is much more clear that God confused the languages of humanity specifically to save them from themselves. Essentially, God confused their languages so He wouldn't have to kill all of them - since God can't allow evil to go unpunished or His 'very specific orders' to be ignored or in this case, openly defied.

What can I add to this video? Here's 10 random personal notes about Illusions of Gaia:
1: Freedan is AWESOME!
2: Shadow is useless.
3: Those vampires suck almost as much balls as The Island of Mu.
4: The boss battle theme is one of the coolest in gaming.
5: The last Red Jewel in the Pyramid is neigh impossible to find for the uninitiated!
6: After 13 years of attempts, the reward for collecting all Red Jewels is both underwhelming and confusing.
7: Dark Flare.
8: Anchor Wat is my favorite level too.
9: I hate that raft.
10: Final Boss may have good music, but I've never faced an easier boss.

And the bonus plus one for those that took the time to read my opinions :D
11: This game, for me, defines a genre of game that works harder on telling you a story, than it does its gameplay mechanics are good graphics. Final Fantasy 4 is another. More games need to do this! And do it well! (I'm looking at you FF13! And all those Quick-Time-Event-focused games!)

+adashingrainbow - But lots of ships are made of very heavy metal, how do they float if they are so heavy?

- I don't know if a ship of gold is possible or not, but it easily could be if we have ships of iron and steel. It's all about water displacement and the buoyancy of the air that displaces the water inside the metal hull. That being said, gold is a soft metal, and that would likely be where you would run into problems. It may not be strong enough to support the weight necessary to displace enough water.... But it wouldn't just sink because it was heavy, it would (probably) break apart under it's own weight. But most ships are very heavy, so that in itself is not the issue, the softness of the gold would be.

To sum up the use of theory for you all: 
A theory is an idea with evidence to back it that has not been tested. (You can't test a video game theory because a video game isn't in this reality, and therefore cannot be tested)
A hypothesis is an idea with evidence to back it that has been tested (in the sense that the hypothesis is made from previous expiremental results)
Lastly, a scientific law is an idea with evidence to back it that has been proven true through a large amount of testing. Hope I helped, and feel free to correct me if I'm wrong.
